ours.name: OURS-v2
ours.output: I'd play Qxd2. Your Qxb2 idea is understandable because it captures the queen on b2, but it misses the more forcing queen move: Qxd2 removes White’s queen right away and also defends your pawn on d5. That is the kind of active queen move you want to try before making a slower capture. How to find it: When your queen is available, do not only ask, “Can I capture?” First ask, “Can my queen capture something more valuable?” Here, Qxd2 wins the queen on d2 and defends your d5 pawn in one move. Takeaway: Before choosing a capture, look for a queen capture that wins a piece and defends something at the same time.

violations.sentence: Your Qxb2 idea is understandable because it captures the queen on b2, but it misses the more forcing queen move: Qxd2 removes White’s queen right away and also defends your pawn on d5.

gpt.name: GPT-5.5
gpt.output: I'd play Qxd2. Instead of grabbing the b2 pawn, take the white queen while you can; it is forcing and much more important than a side pawn. If White recaptures, you have simplified the position and avoided leaving your queen chasing material. Takeaway: When you can make a forcing queen trade, prefer that over grabbing pawns.

claude.name: Claude Opus 4.8
claude.output: I'd play Qxd2. You're already in a strong position, so grabbing that b2-pawn with your queen sends her wandering off to the side of the board where she can get chased or cut off from the action. Instead, trade queens right where they stand — after White recaptures, you're up material and can develop calmly with a move like Bd6, bringing your bishop out and getting ready to castle. When you're ahead, simplifying by trading pieces makes your advantage easier to convert. Takeaway: When you're winning, trade queens and simplify rather than sending your queen off hunting stray pawns.

gemini.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
gemini.output: I'd play Qxd2. When you are already up a full piece, trading queens is the simplest and safest way to secure your victory. Grabbing the pawn on b2 is tempting, but keeping queens on the board gives your opponent unnecessary chances for counterplay. By forcing the queen trade with Qxd2, you eliminate White's most dangerous attacking piece, making it much easier to finish development and win the endgame. Takeaway: When you have a large material advantage, trade queens to simplify the game and reduce your opponent's chances of a comeback.

base.name: BASE
base.output: I'd play Qxd2. The student's move Qxb2 was a mistake because it left the queen vulnerable and didn't develop a strong local attack. Qxd2 is a good move because it attacks the queen on d2, which is undefended, and also develops the queen to d2, putting pressure on the position. After this, the queen is on d2, and the knight is on c6, which is a good development. The key idea is that after Qxd2, the queen is on d2, and the knight is on c6, which is a good position to support the center and attack the pawn on d4. The student missed an opportunity to control the center and attack the key square d4. Takeaway: Focus on controlling the center and attacking key squares with your pieces.

claude.name: Claude Opus 4.8
claude.output: I'd play gxh6. The bishop sitting on h6 is simply hanging, and your pawn on g7 can capture it for free — grabbing a whole piece for nothing is a great deal! With Qf6 you left that free bishop on the board and moved your queen out where it could get chased around, so you missed a straightforward material win. Before making a fancy queen move, always scan the board and ask "is anything just hanging that I can take?" Takeaway: When an enemy piece is undefended and you can safely capture it, take the free material first.

gemini.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
gemini.output: I'd play gxh6. White is threatening an immediate checkmate on g7 using their queen and bishop. Your move, Qf6, tries to block this threat, but unfortunately it just gives your queen away since White can capture it for free. Instead, you can simply capture the bishop with your pawn, which instantly neutralizes the checkmate threat and wins a piece at the same time. Takeaway: When your opponent sets up a dangerous threat, check first if you can simply capture one of the attacking pieces to solve the problem!

base.name: BASE
base.output: I'd play f6. The student's move Qf6 was a mistake because it left the king in a vulnerable position and gave the opponent a clear attack on the king. Instead, f6 is a solid move that prepares to support the king and creates a threat on the g6 square. After f6, the king is safer, and the bishop on h6 becomes a real threat. This move also sets up a good plan for future play. Takeaway: Always look for ways to support the king and create threats that can be used in the future.

ours.name: OURS-v2
ours.output: I'd play exd4. Your Bg4 idea is understandable because it develops a bishop and defends e5, but it misses the more direct central capture. Why it is good: the pawn on d4 is undefended, so you can simply take it and remove that loose piece before you spend another move on development. After exd4, your pawn on c3 is attacked, so you have to deal with that too. This is a clean conversion move: use the loose pawn, make your queen safer, and then choose whether to develop or continue with activity. Takeaway: When you have a loose pawn, take it before spending another move on development.

claude.name: Claude Opus 4.8
claude.output: I'd play exd4. The trouble with Bg4 is that you developed a piece while ignoring the real tension in the center — White could simply grab your e5 pawn, and then your bishop pin doesn't win anything back. By capturing on d4 first you resolve that tension in your favor: after cxd4 you've traded off the center and freed your position, and your pieces (like that same bishop on c8) get better squares to aim at afterward. Settle the center before you go hunting with your bishops. Takeaway: When a central pawn is hanging and there's tension, resolve the center first rather than developing into a tactic that loses the pawn.

By simply capturing with exd4, you resolve that tension safely and force White to spend time recapturing, keeping your position solid. Takeaway: Always check if your central pawns are adequately defended before making a developing move elsewhere on the board.

base.name: BASE
base.output: I'd play Bg4. The student's move was a bit off, but Bg4 is a solid, strategic move. It develops the bishop, attacks the knight on f3, and prepares to take the pawn on e5. After Bg4, the position becomes more balanced, and the bishop is now on a strong square. The key is to follow up with a move that continues the attack on e5 and prepares for a strong follow-up. The mistake here is that the student didn't fully commit to the attack on e5. After Bg4, the best follow-up is to play e5, which is a strong, direct threat. From there, the knight can be pushed to f3, and the queen can support the attack. Takeaway: Focus on committing to the e5 square and using the knight to support that attack.

ours.name: OURS-v2
ours.output: I'd play Bxb6. Your Nd2 idea is understandable because it defends the queen on c2, but it misses the more direct improvement: your bishop on c3 can take the undefended knight on b6. That is a clean intermediate move because it wins a loose piece while also defending your pawn on f2. How to find it: When there is no loose piece hanging, do not automatically defend your queen or bishop. First run this checklist: 1) Which of my pieces is undefended? 2) Can one of my active pieces capture it? 3) After the capture, does that piece also defend something? Here, the knight on b6 is undefended, and Bxb6 wins it while defending f2. Takeaway: Before defending passively, look for an active piece that can win an undefended piece and defend something at the same time.

claude.name: Claude Opus 4.8
claude.output: I'd play Ra3. In this rook endgame you're a pawn down, so passive defense of d6 with your king only lets White keep pressing — instead grab activity with your rook. From a3 your rook eyes the knight and, more importantly, gets behind and beside White's pieces so that even after Rxd6+ your rook stays active and your king can step to h5 with counterplay. The problem with f6 is that it commits a pawn and does nothing to improve your worst-placed asset; when you're defending a pawn-down rook ending, the rook must be the busy piece. Takeaway: In rook endgames, active pieces save the day far more often than clinging to a pawn.

gemini.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
gemini.output: I'd play Ra3. Pushing your pawn to f6 is a bit passive and creates unnecessary structural weaknesses. In these types of endgames, the activity of your rook is often your most powerful asset. By playing Ra3, you immediately seize the initiative by attacking the knight on f3, forcing White to react. Even if White decides to grab your d6 pawn with a check, your king can safely step up to h5, and your highly active rook ensures you maintain excellent counterplay. Takeaway: In endgames, prioritize active piece play and creating immediate threats over slow, defensive pawn moves.
